concerns.location: plan.md:Phase‑wise Plan & FR/SC Mapping table – Phase 6 (Aggregate Analyses)
concerns.text: No power analysis is provided for the aggregate prevalence estimate (binomial test H₀ : π = 0.05). With the planned minimum corpus (≈100 summaries) the study may be severely under‑powered to detect a modest deviation from 5 % inconsistency, risking a Type II error.

The extraction step assumes that any reported “lift %” can be directly converted to an absolute difference without explicit conversion rules. This may mis‑measure effect sizes when the source reports odds ratios, relative risk, or uses different baselines, threatening construct validity of the `effect_size` variable.

concerns.location: plan.md:FR‑004 description (referenced in Phase 4 – Reconstruction and Phase 5 – Consistency Flagging)
concerns.text: The inconsistency thresholds (absolute p‑value difference > 0.05, relative effect‑size difference > 5 %) are introduced without justification or sensitivity analysis. Arbitrary cut‑offs can inflate false‑positive inconsistency rates and undermine the validity of the prevalence claim.

The synthetic validation dataset is generated programmatically with known ground‑truth statistics, but it does not model the diverse reporting quirks (rounded p‑values, missing confidence intervals, mixed effect‑size units) observed in real‑world A/B test summaries. Consequently, precision/recall measured on this set may not generalize, limiting external validity of the detector’s performance claims.

Aggregate analyses treat all summaries as a homogeneous population, yet source‑wise reporting practices (e.g., tech blogs vs. academic repositories) can differ systematically. Without modeling source as a stratification factor or adjusting for such heterogeneity, confounding may bias the overall inconsistency proportion and the subsequent binomial test.
